perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with the laws of England. The Parties hereby consent to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of England and consent that all personal service of process be made by certified mail or registered mail directed to the address stated herein and service so made shall be deemed to be completed upon actual receipt thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with the laws of England. The Parties hereby consent to the jurisdiction of the courts of England, but service of process may only be perfected via personal service, delivered directly to an officer of the company.
perturbation.explanation: By restricting the method of service of process to 'personal service' only, the contract conflicts with standard procedural rules for service under English law, which allows for alternative methods of service under certain conditions. This introduces uncertainty and could invalidate service if personal service cannot be achieved.
perturbation.law_explanation: CPR Part 6 outlines various methods of serving a claim form, including methods other than personal service. The modified text restricts service to only personal service, contradicting the flexibility provided by the CPR. See Section 6.15(1) and Section 6.9 on how to serve a company.

perturbation.justification: The modified text restricts service of process to 'personal service,' while the original contract allowed for service by certified or registered mail. The Law Explanation highlights that CPR Part 6 provides various methods of serving a claim form, not limited to personal service. Scraped Law Snippet 2 includes 'PART 6 SERVICE OF DOCUMENTS I GENERAL RULES ABOUT SERVICE' and lists multiple sections, including 6.2 'Methods of service—general', indicating that English law allows for methods of service beyond just personal service. The modified text directly contradicts this by limiting service to only personal delivery, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information means any and all information and know-how that the Discloser and/or its Group Parties and/or its agents may from time to time disclose to the Recipient or the Recipient’s MUTUAL NON-DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT ANNEX 2 SAMPLE NON-DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT I ELAN MOBILE MONEY ASSESSMENT AND CONTRACTING GUIDE 3 Group Parties during the course of the Matter or which otherwise becomes known to the Recipient either during the course of the Matter or during the course of any previous discussions, negotiations or relationship with the Discloser in relation to the Matter, whether orally, in writing, digitally, in the form of machine readable code or embodied in hardware or any other physical medium which relates to the business, including but not limited to the business plan of the Discloser or their Group Parties, data, know-how, designs, illustrations, drawings, photographs, illustrations, notes, memoranda, terms of business, financial information, financial projections, financial records, customers and suppliers, sales and marketing information, spreadsheets, specifications, technical information and computer software of the Discloser and/or its Group Parties;
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information means any and all information and know-how that the Discloser and/or its Group Parties and/or its agents may from time to time disclose to the Recipient or the Recipient’s Group Parties during the course of the Matter or which otherwise becomes known to the Recipient either during the course of the Matter or during the course of any previous discussions, negotiations or relationship with the Discloser in relation to the Matter, whether orally, in writing, digitally, in the form of machine readable code or embodied in hardware or any other physical medium which relates to the business, including but not limited to the business plan of the Discloser or their Group Parties, data, designs, illustrations, drawings, photographs, illustrations, notes, memoranda, terms of business, financial information, financial projections, financial records, customers and suppliers, sales and marketing information, spreadsheets, specifications, technical information and computer software of the Discloser and/or its Group Parties;
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'know-how' from the definition of 'Confidential Information', the contract potentially contradicts the legal protection afforded to trade secrets and confidential information under English law. 'Know-how' is a key element in establishing a trade secret, and its removal weakens the enforceability of the NDA.
perturbation.law_explanation: Regulation 2 of the Trade Secrets Regulations defines 'trade secret' in a manner that encompasses 'know-how'. By excluding 'know-how' from the contract's definition of 'Confidential Information', the contract provides less protection than afforded by the law concerning trade secrets.

perturbation.justification: The removal of 'know-how' from the definition of Confidential Information in the changed text directly contradicts the protection intended for trade secrets under English law. The Law Explanation states that 'Regulation 2 of the Trade Secrets Regulations defines 'trade secret' in a manner that encompasses 'know-how'.' While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is unavailable, Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to 'The Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018', indicating the legal framework in place for trade secret protection. The Perturbation Explanation further emphasizes that 'know-how' is a key element in establishing a trade secret, and its removal weakens the enforceability of the NDA. Therefore, the modification creates a legal contradiction by providing less protection than the law affords to trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations of each of the Parties with respect to Confidential Information shall extend for a period of three (3) years following the date of disclosure of that Confidential Information; provided, however, that confidentiality obligations herein shall survive for a period of three (3) years from the date of termination of this Agreement;
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of each of the Parties with respect to Confidential Information shall extend indefinitely following the date of disclosure of that Confidential Information; provided, however, that confidentiality obligations herein shall terminate upon the sale or liquidation of the disclosing party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the duration of confidentiality from a fixed term to 'indefinitely' with a termination trigger based on the disclosing party's sale or liquidation creates ambiguity and potential conflict with standard interpretations of reasonable duration for confidentiality obligations. Courts are unlikely to enforce indefinite confidentiality, especially with such a contingency, as it could be deemed an unreasonable restraint of trade.
perturbation.contradicted_law: English common law on restraint of trade
perturbation.law_citation: English common law - Restraint of Trade
perturbation.law_explanation: English common law requires that restraints of trade be reasonable in duration and scope. While confidentiality agreements can be a legitimate restraint, an indefinite term is likely to be viewed as unreasonable, particularly if tied to events like the sale or liquidation of a company, which is difficult to justify to protect the confidential information. The link directs to a case about Restraint of Trade.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: restraint of trade | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you restraint of trade A restraint of trade is any activity that tends to limit a party's ability to enter into transactions. The term is most commonly used in the context of government antitrust regulation. For example, federally, 15 U.S.C. § 1 prohibits “[e]very contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations.” For example, in American Needle, Inc. v. Nat’l Football League , the U.S. Supreme Court held that licensing activities for individual teams’ intellectual property conducted through a corporation separate from the teams with its own management [i.e. the NFL] fell within a possible restraint of trade covered under 15 U.S.C. § 1. Many states also have their own local restraints on anti-competitive business activity. For example, Massachusetts General Law Ch. 93 § 4 states “[e]very contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce in the commonwealth shall be unlawful.” [Last reviewed in April of 2025 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities unfair competition wex definitions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts the principle that restraints of trade must be reasonable in duration and scope. The original text had a fixed duration (3 years), while the changed text extends the confidentiality obligations indefinitely, only terminating upon the sale or liquidation of the disclosing party. According to the Law Explanation, an indefinite term is likely viewed as unreasonable, particularly tied to events like the sale or liquidation of a company. The provided snippet from Wex defines restraint of trade as any activity that tends to limit a party's ability to enter into transactions. By imposing an indefinite confidentiality obligation that may terminate only upon the sale or liquidation of a company, the changed text creates a potentially unreasonable restraint on trade, since the disclosing party may not be able to transact freely.

perturbation.original_text: Assignment. Neither Party shall assign this Agreement to a third party without the other Party’s prior written consent, which such consent will not be unreasonably withheld, denied, conditioned or delayed; provided, however, that each Party may assign the rights and obligations to any Group Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Assignment. Neither Party shall assign this Agreement to a third party without the other Party’s prior written consent, which such consent may be withheld at the sole discretion of the non-assigning party; provided, however, that each Party may assign the rights and obligations to any Group Party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the assignment clause to allow consent to be withheld at the 'sole discretion' of the non-assigning party potentially contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing in contract law. English courts may scrutinize such clauses if they are exercised in an arbitrary or capricious manner.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied duty of good faith in English contract law
perturbation.law_citation: Yam Seng Pte Ltd v International Trade Corporation Ltd [2013] EWHC 111 (QB)
perturbation.law_explanation: While English law does not have a universally implied duty of good faith, the case of Yam Seng Pte Ltd v International Trade Corporation Ltd [2013] EWHC 111 (QB) suggests a willingness to imply such a duty in certain commercial contracts. A 'sole discretion' clause is more likely to be challenged if the withholding of consent is deemed to be commercially unreasonable or in bad faith.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'will not be unreasonably withheld, denied, conditioned or delayed' to 'may be withheld at the sole discretion of the non-assigning party' directly contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation. While the Scraped Law Snippet 1 focuses on legislation resources and doesn't directly address the duty of good faith, the Law Explanation references the Yam Seng case, which suggests that 'sole discretion' clauses can be challenged if deemed commercially unreasonable or in bad faith. The original text implies a reasonable standard for withholding consent, whereas the modified text eliminates this requirement, potentially enabling arbitrary denial, which introduces a legal contradiction based on the implied duty of good faith.

perturbation.original_text: Third parties cannot benefit from this Agreement under The Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999.
perturbation.changed_text: Third parties may benefit from this Agreement under The Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999, but only with the express written consent of both Parties.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the clause to allow third-party beneficiaries with consent contradicts the original intent of excluding the application of the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999. While it adds a condition (written consent), it introduces the possibility of third-party rights, which could create ambiguity and potential litigation if not handled carefully.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999, Section 1(1)
perturbation.law_citation: Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999, Section 1(1)
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 1(1) of the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999 states that a third party may enforce a term of a contract if the contract expressly provides that he may, or if the term purports to confer a benefit on him. The modified text, while requiring consent, opens the door to third-party rights under the Act, which the original clause explicitly excluded.

perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly excludes third parties from benefiting under the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999. The changed text introduces the possibility for third parties to benefit with written consent. The Law Explanation confirms that the modified text opens the door to third-party rights under the Act, which the original clause explicitly excluded. The change directly contradicts the original intent by allowing third-party benefits, which the original clause forbade. Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999 is an active piece of legislation.
